hints.junior: When a form fails validation, the user's working memory is already full — they just filled out 8 fields and hit submit. Showing a generic 'Something went wrong' at the top forces them to re-parse everything. Inline errors next to the specific field reduce that reload to a single piece of information.
hints.mid: Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller, 1988) distinguishes intrinsic load (the task itself), extraneous load (bad UI design), and germane load (learning). Error messages add extraneous load — the user's attention and working memory are redirected from their goal to debugging your UI. Inline, specific errors are the primary way to reduce extraneous load in form flows.
hints.staff: The schema/error mapping pattern: every API error response should map to a specific field or a specific, actionable message. A 422 with `{ field: 'email', code: 'ALREADY_EXISTS' }` enables a precise inline error. A generic 500 with no field mapping forces a summary error — this is an API design failure, not a UI failure, but the UX cost is paid by the user.

hints.junior: Submit the broken form with errors. Notice the error summary at the top — you have to read 4 error messages, then scroll down to find each field. Now try the fixed form. Errors appear immediately next to the offending field with a clear fix.
hints.mid: The demo logs your time-to-resubmit for both forms. The inline error form is typically 60–70% faster on resubmit, because users don't have to re-parse and re-locate — they can fix and move on.
hints.staff: Error recovery has two sub-problems: discoverability (user notices the error) and actionability (user knows how to fix it). Inline errors with specific, actionable copy solve both. 'Invalid email' solves neither — 'Enter a valid email like name@example.com' solves both.

hints.junior: The concept chapter is the principle — read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
hints.mid: Connect the principle to your component API — what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
hints.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

hints.junior: Skim the code patterns here — you can copy the structure even if the syntax is new.
hints.mid: Compare broken vs fixed implementation — the diff is usually smaller than you expect.
hints.staff: Check edge cases: SSR, hydration, design tokens, and high-contrast mode.

anecdotes.junior: I shipped a contact form that showed all validation errors in a red box at the top of the page. Support tickets for 'form doesn't work' increased 5x on mobile — users were submitting, seeing the error box scroll out of view, and thinking the page had broken. Inline errors eliminated all those tickets.
anecdotes.mid: The pattern I see most: `catch (err) { setError('Something went wrong. Please try again.') }`. This is the worst error message possible. Always map errors to specific, actionable copy before they reach the UI. Even 'We couldn't save your changes — please check your connection and try again' is massively better.
anecdotes.staff: Error boundaries in React are a different problem than form validation errors. Error boundaries catch rendering exceptions — they're a last resort for catastrophic failures. Form validation errors are expected flow states and should be part of the component's state machine, not caught by an error boundary.

sections.junior: This case is about cognitive load during error recovery — why error summaries at the top of forms make things worse, and why inline errors fix them.
sections.mid: We're covering Cognitive Load Theory, extraneous vs intrinsic load in form flows, inline vs summary error patterns, and actionable error copy.
sections.staff: We're covering Sweller 1988, the schema/error mapping API contract, the two sub-problems of error recovery (discoverability and actionability), and React error boundary scope.
